pages.raw_text:
WASHINGTON, July 19.-Panic
and decentralization nave been
caused among settlers on the gov
eminent reclamation projects by
an order just issued by Secretary
Ballinger of the department of the
interior. This sentiment is re-
Elected in thousands of letters
which are now pouring into Wash
ington


The trouble grows out of a new
reversal of a GarfieldRoosevelt
policy. A legal objection has been
found to one of the practices insti-
tuted by Secretary Garfield and
Chief F. H. Newell of the reclama-
ton service.


Here is the situation:


People who take up the govern
ment reclamation land undertake
to pay for it in installments. Most
of these people have very little
money. The settler may have a
team of horses and enough money
to get one crop in the ground, but
when he has done this and made
his first payment on the land he
has little left.


But the settler is willing to
work; and he generally has plenty
of time to work. If he could do
some work digging bitches and
helping to finish --e reclamation
project it would help him a lot in
paying for his land That was the
situation so far as the settlers were
concerned


Now, on the other hand the gov
eminent wanted work done. The
government was very anxious to
make use of the men and their
teams. So Secretary Garfield and
Mr.. ewell figured out plan by
which the government and the set
\ tier could cooperate. They said
TO the settlers: You turn in and
do this work; we will keep tab and
give you credit and will issue to
you certificates for the amount of
work done. Then we will accept
those certificates on account of
what you owe on the government
land"


This scheme pleased the settlers
very much. They went to work
and have been paying for their
lands in part by digging bitches.
hauling dirt and doing other work
which the government wanted
done. 1t gave employment to those
who needed employment and no.
body was harmed.


But Secretary Ballinger now says
the whole thing is Illegal" On
his own motion he asked the attor-
ney general for an opinion inter-
preting the law and has received 20
typewriter pages from Mr.. Wick
ersham declaring that the law does
not warrant the issue of certifi
Gates nor the acceptance of such
certificates in payment for the
land.


Probably Mr.. Wickersham is cor
rect. No doubt congress did neg
lect to provide specifically for such
a condition of affairs, but Mr.. Bal
linger is the first to find any rea-
son for interfering with the work
ings of a plan which resulted in
